facts.text: Snowboarding became an official Olympic sport at the 1998 Winter Games in Nagano, Japan.
Sherman Poppen invented the 'Snurfer' in 1965 by bolting two skis together for his daughter.
Shaun White has won three Olympic gold medals in the halfpipe, more than any other snowboarder.
A standard Olympic halfpipe is 22 feet (6.7 meters) tall with walls called 'transitions.'
Burton Snowboards, founded in 1977, is the largest snowboard manufacturer in the world.
The McTwist is an inverted 540-degree rotation originally adapted from skateboarding.
Chloe Kim became the youngest woman to win an Olympic snowboarding gold medal at age 17.
Mammoth Mountain in California is home to one of the most famous terrain parks in the world.
Splitboarding uses a board that separates into two ski-like halves for uphill travel in the backcountry.
The X Games first featured snowboarding in 1997 at Big Bear Lake, California.
Terje Haakonsen boycotted the 1998 Olympics, protesting the FIS's control over snowboarding.
Freestyle snowboard boots are softer and more flexible than alpine racing boots.
Travis Rice's film 'The Art of Flight' (2011) is considered one of the greatest snowboard films ever made.
Slopestyle snowboarding involves performing tricks on a course of rails, jumps, and other features.
Whistler Blackcomb in British Columbia has over 8,100 acres of skiable terrain popular with snowboarders.
Jake Burton Carpenter, founder of Burton Snowboards, is often called the 'father of modern snowboarding.'
A 'double cork' trick involves two off-axis flips combined with rotations.
Snowboard cross (boardercross) features four to six riders racing simultaneously down a course with jumps and banked turns.
The Dew Tour is a major action sports competition that features snowboarding events each winter.
Avalanche transceivers, probes, and shovels are essential safety gear for backcountry snowboarding.
Tom Sims, a skateboard pioneer, built one of the first snowboards in 1963 as a shop class project.
Parallel giant slalom was removed from the Olympic program after the 2018 PyeongChang Games.
Lib Tech, based in Washington state, is known for its environmentally friendly snowboard manufacturing.
Red Gerard won the first gold medal of the 2018 PyeongChang Olympics in men's slopestyle at age 17.
A superpipe has walls that are 22 feet high, compared to a standard halfpipe's 18-foot walls.
Snowboarding culture emerged heavily from skateboarding and surfing traditions in the 1980s.
The Natural Selection Tour, created by Travis Rice, takes place in big-mountain backcountry terrain.
Heli-boarding involves using a helicopter to access remote, untracked powder runs.
Jamie Anderson won the first-ever Olympic gold medal in women's slopestyle in 2014.
The FIS Snowboard World Cup circuit includes events in halfpipe, slopestyle, snowboard cross, and parallel disciplines.
Many ski resorts banned snowboarding in the 1980s, viewing it as dangerous and disruptive.
Powder boards are wider and often have a directional shape with a set-back stance for floating in deep snow.
Ayumu Hirano landed the first-ever triple cork in Olympic halfpipe competition at the 2022 Beijing Games.
Breckenridge, Colorado is one of the most popular snowboard destinations in North America.
Snowboard wax reduces friction between the base of the board and the snow surface.
Mark McMorris has won more X Games snowboarding medals than any other male athlete.
The 'method' grab is one of the most iconic and stylish tricks in snowboarding.
Snowboard bindings come in three main types: strap-in, rear-entry, and step-on.
Torah Bright won Olympic gold in the halfpipe at the 2010 Vancouver Games.
Jackson Hole Mountain Resort in Wyoming is renowned for its steep, expert-level backcountry terrain.
A 'cab' trick is a switch (fakie) frontside rotation, named after skateboarder Steve Caballero.
Snowboard cross was added to the Winter Olympics at the 2006 Turin Games.
The US Open of Snowboarding, held at Vail, is one of the oldest and most prestigious snowboard competitions.
GNU Snowboards is a sister company of Lib Tech and is known for its asymmetric board designs.
Early snowboards had no metal edges, making them difficult to control on hard-packed snow.
Shaun White scored a perfect 100 at the 2012 X Games in the superpipe.
Laax in Switzerland is home to one of Europe's largest and best terrain parks.
A 'frontside 1080' involves three full rotations while facing downhill at takeoff.
The International Snowboard Federation (ISF) was founded in 1990 to govern competitive snowboarding.
Snowboard film production company Standard Films released the influential 'TB' series for over two decades.
Camber, rocker, and flat are the three main profile types for snowboard bases.
Ross Powers, Danny Kass, and J.J. Thomas swept the men's halfpipe podium for the USA at the 2002 Olympics.
Freestyle boards are typically shorter, more flexible, and have a true twin shape for riding switch.
The first Snurfer competition was held in Muskegon, Michigan in 1968.
Jones Snowboards was founded by legendary big-mountain rider Jeremy Jones in 2009.
Snowboard magazines like Transworld Snowboarding and Snowboarder helped define the sport's culture in the 1990s.
Big air snowboarding made its Olympic debut at the 2018 PyeongChang Games.
The 'Indy grab' involves grabbing the toe edge between the bindings with the back hand.
Stratton Mountain in Vermont was one of the first resorts to allow snowboarding in 1983.
Chloe Kim landed back-to-back 1080s in the halfpipe at the 2018 Olympics, a first for women's competition.
Snowboard boots use a lacing system that can be traditional laces, speed laces, or BOA dials.
The Rider's Poll by Snowboarder Magazine is a prestigious annual award voted on by professional riders.
Burton's 'Channel' binding system uses a single track instead of traditional insert patterns.
The term 'goofy' refers to riding with the right foot forward, while 'regular' means left foot forward.
Terje Haakonsen set the world record for highest air out of a halfpipe at 9.8 meters in 2007.
Anna Gasser became the first woman to land a cab triple underflip 1260 in competition.
Snowboard cross courses typically feature rollers, berms, jumps, and narrow sections.
The 'stalefish' grab is performed by reaching behind the back leg to grab the heel edge.
Alta and Deer Valley in Utah remain among the last major resorts that still ban snowboarding.
Snowboarding has influenced mainstream fashion with brands like Volcom, DC Shoes, and 686.
The FIS took over governance of competitive snowboarding from the ISF in the late 1990s.
A 'butter' is a ground trick where the rider presses the nose or tail of the board while spinning.
Zeb Powell is known for his creative, unconventional style that blends street skating with snowboarding.
Sintered bases are faster and more durable than extruded bases but require more maintenance.
The Winter X Games have been held in Aspen, Colorado since 2002.
Halfpipe snowboarding requires riders to perform tricks while launching out of a U-shaped channel of snow.
Craig Kelly, a four-time world champion, is considered one of the greatest snowboarders of all time.
An all-mountain board is designed to handle varied terrain from groomed runs to powder to park.
The 'chicken salad' grab involves reaching between the legs and grabbing the heel edge with the leading hand.
Snowboarding generates over $1 billion annually in equipment sales worldwide.
Shaun White retired from competitive snowboarding after the 2022 Beijing Olympics.
The 'ollie' in snowboarding, borrowed from skateboarding, is the foundation for many aerial tricks.
Nicolas Huber landed a backside quadruple cork 1800 in 2023, one of the most rotations ever in competition.
Nitro Snowboards, founded in 1990 in Seattle, is one of the original rider-owned snowboard companies.
Terrain park features are rated green circle, blue square, and black diamond like ski runs.
Snowboard edges are made of steel and are sharpened to help grip on ice and hard-packed snow.
The 'nose grab' involves reaching forward and grabbing the tip of the snowboard.
Mark McMorris survived a severe backcountry crash in 2017 that left him with multiple broken bones.
The Burton US Open has been running since 1982, making it one of snowboarding's longest-running events.
Ride Snowboards, founded in 1992, became one of the top-selling board brands in the late 1990s.
A 'jib' in snowboarding refers to riding on non-snow surfaces like rails, boxes, and logs.
Mammoth Mountain's Unbound Terrain Parks feature over 100 jumps and jibs across multiple parks.
Snowboard sidecut radius determines how tight or wide the board's natural turn arc will be.
Craig Kelly died in an avalanche while heli-boarding in British Columbia in January 2003.
A 'cork' trick involves going off-axis during a spin, creating a tilted rotation.
Women's snowboard halfpipe was added to the Olympics in 2002 at Salt Lake City.
The first major snowboarding video game was 'Cool Boarders' for PlayStation in 1996.
Magnetraction by Lib Tech uses a wavy edge design for better grip on icy conditions.
A volume-shifted board is shorter but wider than traditional boards, designed for powder performance.
Danny Davis founded the 'Peace Park,' an artistic terrain park session held annually at various resorts.
The 'roast beef' grab involves reaching between the legs from behind and grabbing the heel edge.
Capita Snowboards is based in Austria and manufactures boards in its own clean-energy 'Mothership' factory.
Snowboard wax comes in temperature-specific formulas to match snow conditions.
Kazu Kokubo is one of Japan's most famous snowboarders, known for his massive airs and stylish riding.
Parallel giant slalom features two riders racing side by side through gates on separate courses.
The first snowboard halfpipe was hand-dug by riders at Tahoe City, California in 1979.
A 'melon' grab is performed by reaching across the body to grab the heel edge between the bindings.
Snowboarding was the fastest-growing sport in the United States during the 1990s.
Gretchen Bleiler was a dominant force in women's halfpipe, winning X Games gold multiple times.
Board flex is rated on a scale from 1 (softest) to 10 (stiffest), affecting performance in different conditions.
The 'tail grab' involves reaching behind and grabbing the tail of the snowboard during a jump.
Vail Resorts owns several of the most popular snowboarding mountains under the Epic Pass.
Snowboard stomp pads provide traction for the back foot when skating on flat ground.
Anna Gasser won the first Olympic gold medal in women's big air at PyeongChang 2018.
The 'backside rodeo' combines a backside rotation with an inverted backflip.
Many professional snowboarders earn significant income from sponsorships and video parts rather than competition prize money.
K2 Snowboards introduced the first twin-tip snowboard in the early 1990s.
Snowboard helmets have become standard in competition and are increasingly worn by recreational riders.
The 'Japan air' involves tucking the board behind the rider while grabbing the toe edge.
Niseko in Japan is famous for its deep, consistent powder snow, attracting snowboarders worldwide.
The Fédération Internationale de Ski (FIS) governs Olympic snowboarding events.
Sage Kotsenburg won the first-ever Olympic slopestyle gold medal with a trick he invented on the spot.
Effective edge length is the portion of the board's edge that actually contacts the snow during a turn.
The 'flatground trick' category includes butters, presses, and ground-based spins without jumps.
Snowboarding street-style riding involves hitting urban features like handrails, stairs, and ledges.
Capita's 'Defenders of Awesome' is one of the best-selling freestyle boards of all time.
The 'wildcat' is a backflip performed on a straight jump, rotating head-over-heels along the board's axis.
Lake Louise in Alberta, Canada hosts FIS Snowboard World Cup cross events.
P-tex is the plastic material used for snowboard base repairs.
Hannah Teter won Olympic halfpipe gold for the USA at the 2006 Turin Games.
A 'switch' trick is performed while riding in the opposite of the rider's natural stance.
The 'Crippler' is an inverted 540 performed in the halfpipe, where the rider rotates backside.
Snowboard goggles use different lens tints for varying light conditions from bright sun to overcast.
Jeremy Jones's film trilogy 'Deeper, Further, Higher' showcased remote backcountry snowboarding.
The '50-50' is the most basic rail trick, where the rider slides straight along a rail or box.
Asymmetric sidecuts on snowboards account for the natural difference between heelside and toeside turns.
Shaun White's nickname 'The Flying Tomato' came from his distinctive red hair.
Snowboard racing gates are triangular and flexible, designed to bend when hit.
Burton's 'Step On' binding system allows riders to click into bindings without using straps.
The 2022 Beijing Olympics halfpipe final featured the most technically advanced tricks in Olympic history.
Powder riding typically uses a set-back stance, shifting the rider's weight toward the tail of the board.
The 'boardslide' involves sliding perpendicular across a rail or box with the board at 90 degrees.
Zinc oxide sunscreen is essential for snowboarding due to intense UV radiation at high altitudes.
The Natural Selection Tour format features qualifiers and head-to-head heats in natural terrain.
Kelly Clark won 78 World Cup podiums, more than any halfpipe snowboarder in history.
A 'carve' uses the board's sidecut to make clean, arcing turns without skidding.
Snowboard flex patterns can be uniform, progressive, or directional depending on the board's design.
The US Snowboard Team is managed by USA Snowboard, a division of US Ski & Snowboard.
Snowboard film crews often spend weeks camping in remote backcountry zones to capture footage.
The 'tail press' involves shifting weight to the tail and lifting the nose off the snow.
Jake Burton Carpenter passed away on November 20, 2019, leaving a massive legacy in the sport.
Snowboard cross became known for exciting crashes and unexpected results due to its race format.
The 'tamedog' is a front flip performed by springing off the nose of the board.
Union Bindings is one of the leading binding manufacturers, popular among freestyle and all-mountain riders.
The World Snowboard Tour was established to unify international snowboard competition circuits.
A twin-tip snowboard has identical nose and tail shapes, making it equally effective riding forward or switch.
Hakuba Valley in Japan hosted the 1998 Olympic snowboarding events.
Snowboard cross courses drop 200-250 meters in elevation over about 1,000 meters of course length.
The 'frontflip' or 'wildcat' can be performed off natural terrain features in backcountry riding.
Bataleon Snowboards pioneered Triple Base Technology, which lifts the contact points for easier turn initiation.
Snowboard instructor certification is offered by organizations like AASI in the United States.
The 'lipslide' involves approaching a rail and passing the board over it before sliding.
Volkl, traditionally a ski company, has also produced competitive snowboards for racing.
The US Open halfpipe is one of the few events where riders can qualify without FIS points.
Lindsey Jacobellis won Olympic snowboard cross gold at age 36 at the 2022 Beijing Games.
Snowboard base grinding machines level and structure the base for optimal glide.
The 'method air' was popularized by skateboarder Neil Blender and adapted for snowboarding by Terry Kidwell.
Riders use wrist guards to prevent injuries, as wrist fractures are the most common snowboard injury.
The Air & Style contest in Innsbruck, Austria combined big air snowboarding with a music festival.
A directional board has a longer nose and shorter tail, designed primarily for riding in one direction.
Mark McMorris landed the first-ever backside triple cork 1440 in competition in 2011.
Snowboard tuning involves waxing, edge sharpening, and base repair for optimal performance.
The 'kicker' is a term for any jump feature in a terrain park or backcountry.
Snowboarding was considered a 'rebellious' alternative to skiing throughout the 1980s and 1990s.
Park City Mountain Resort in Utah has one of the largest terrain parks in North America.
The 'nollie' is the snowboard equivalent of a skateboarding nollie, popping off the nose.
Seb Toots (Sébastien Toutant) won the first-ever Olympic big air gold medal in 2018.
Snowboard halfpipe walls are shaped by specialized pipe-cutting machines called 'pipe dragons.'
The 'hand plant' involves grabbing the lip of the halfpipe and doing a handstand before re-entering.
Salomon entered the snowboard market in the late 1990s, bringing ski technology to snowboard design.
Snowboard competitions use a judging system that scores amplitude, difficulty, execution, and variety.
The 'shifty' involves rotating the board mid-air and then rotating it back before landing.
Rome Snowboards was founded by riders Paul Maravetz and Josh Reid in 2001.
Burton's first factory was in a barn in Londonderry, Vermont.
A snowboard's waist width must be matched to boot size to prevent toe and heel drag during turns.
The 'pretzel' is a rail trick where the rider spins onto the rail in one direction and off in the opposite.
Torstein Horgmo is known for his creative freestyle riding and social media presence.
Alpine snowboarding uses hard-shell boots and plate bindings for aggressive carving and racing.
The 'drop-in' refers to entering the halfpipe from the top deck.
Snowboard cross was almost removed from the Olympics but was retained due to its viewer popularity.
Endeavor Snowboards, based in British Columbia, is an independent rider-owned brand.
A '180' is the simplest rotational trick, involving a half-rotation in the air.
The Burton Riglet program teaches kids as young as three years old to snowboard using a reel system.
Snowboard impact shorts have padding to protect hips and tailbone during falls.
Peter Line is credited as the first person to land a double cork in snowboarding.
The 'nose press' involves shifting weight forward to ride on just the nose of the board.
Revelstoke Mountain Resort in BC has the most vertical drop in North America at 1,713 meters.
Snowboard leashes, once mandatory at many resorts, are increasingly no longer required.
A 'gap jump' involves launching over an obstacle like a road, creek, or cliff band.
The X Games introduced the 'Real Snow' video competition for urban snowboarding.
Iouri Podladtchikov won Olympic halfpipe gold in 2014 with a move he called the 'YOLO Flip.'
Snowboard camber provides edge hold and pop, while rocker offers float and playfulness.
Never Summer is a Colorado-based snowboard company known for its proprietary Carbonium technology.
The 'air-to-fakie' involves going straight up out of the halfpipe and landing backward.
Goggles with spherical lenses reduce distortion compared to cylindrical lenses.
The average professional snowboarder's career spans about 10-15 years.
Mikkel Bang is a Norwegian snowboarder known for his stylish backcountry riding and film parts.
The size of a snowboard is primarily determined by the rider's weight, not height.
Snowboard racing was more popular in Europe than North America during the sport's early years.
The 'lay-back slide' is a style move where the rider leans back and touches the snow with one hand.
YES Snowboards, co-founded by DCP and Romain De Marchi, focuses on progressive board shapes.
The USASA (United States of America Snowboard and Freeski Association) organizes amateur competitions.
A 'flat spin' is a rotation in the horizontal plane without inverted movement.
Burton acquired Channel Islands Surfboards to cross-pollinate surf and snow design ideas.
Snowboard stance width and angle settings dramatically affect a rider's comfort and performance.
The 'eggplant' is an inverted hand plant in the halfpipe where the frontside hand grabs the coping.
Snowboarding participation peaked around 2010 with over 8 million riders in the US.
Arbor Snowboards uses sustainable wood cores and eco-friendly manufacturing processes.
A 'tripod' is a ground trick where the rider balances on the nose and one hand.
Jake Burton competed in the very first National Snurfer Championship in 1979 with his own design.
Snowboard halfpipe judges look for amplitude, difficulty, variety, progression, and execution.
The 'tindy' grab (simultaneously grabbing tail and Indy) is generally considered poor style in snowboarding.
Mt. Baker in Washington state receives some of the heaviest snowfall in North America, averaging over 600 inches per year.
Snowboard bindings have highbacks that support the rider's calf and influence heelside leverage.
The FIS Snowboard World Championships are held every two years in odd-numbered years.
Scotty James won three consecutive halfpipe world titles for Australia.
A 'poke' refers to straightening one or both legs during a grab to add style.
Snowboard touring or splitboarding has grown dramatically since 2015 as backcountry access tools improved.
The 'frontside 360' is a fundamental aerial trick where the rider faces downhill at the start of the spin.
Snowboard helmet technology includes MIPS (Multi-directional Impact Protection System) for rotational forces.
Danny Kass won back-to-back Olympic silver medals in halfpipe in 2002 and 2006.
Custom snowboard graphics have been an art form since the sport's earliest days, often featuring bold, rebellious imagery.
A 'noseblunt' is a rail trick where the rider slides on the nose of the board at an angle.
Snowboard film production company Absinthe Films has been making influential movies since 2000.
The '720' involves two full rotations and is a standard trick in competitive halfpipe.
Snowboard cross was introduced as a test event at the 2003 FIS World Championships.
The 'rocket air' involves grabbing the nose with both hands while the body extends behind.
Ross Powers won Olympic halfpipe gold at the 2002 Salt Lake City Games.
Snowboard bindings use either a 4-hole or channel mounting system to attach to the board.
Stale Sandbech is known for winning slopestyle events and his appearance in snowboard films.
A 'run' in competition is a single pass down the course or through the halfpipe.
Snowboard boot stiffness ratings range from soft (1-3) for park to stiff (8-10) for all-mountain and freeride.
The 'seatbelt' grab involves reaching across the body over the front knee to grab the nose.
Chad's Gap at Brighton Resort in Utah was one of the most famous backcountry jumps until it was filled in.
Snowboard goggle technology includes anti-fog coatings, UV protection, and quick-change lens systems.
Philipp Schoch won Olympic parallel giant slalom gold in both 2002 and 2006.
A 'rodeo' flip combines a frontside or backside rotation with an inverted flip.
The 'skate banana' by Lib Tech was one of the first mass-produced rocker boards.
Snowboarding at night under floodlights is popular at many resorts and is called 'night riding.'
The 'bluntslide' involves ollieing onto a rail with the tail of the board over the feature.
Professional snowboarders often have shorter competitive careers than skiers due to impact injuries.
In 2018, Red Gerard almost missed his Olympic event after oversleeping.
Snowboard films are a key part of the culture, with annual releases from major production companies.
A 'pow slash' is a stylish spray of powder made by doing a quick turn in deep snow.
Snowboard cross teams events were added to the 2010 Vancouver Olympic program.
The 'mute grab' involves grabbing the toe edge between the bindings with the leading hand.
CAPiTA (formerly Capita) Snowboards operates the Mothership, one of the most advanced snowboard factories.
Kelly Clark won the first women's Olympic halfpipe gold medal in 2002 at Salt Lake City.
The 'duck stance' has a positive angle on the front binding and negative angle on the back for switch riding.
Snowboard mogul riding is uncommon in competition but is practiced by advanced riders.
The 'tweak' is the exaggeration of a grab by pulling the board in an extreme direction.
Flow bindings pioneered the rear-entry design for faster entry and exit from bindings.
Snowboard injuries most commonly affect the wrists, followed by the shoulders and ankles.
The first Burton snowboard was called the 'Burton Backhill,' produced in 1977.
Marcus Kleveland landed the first quad cork 1800 in snowboarding history in 2017.
The 'bonk' involves deliberately hitting a non-standard feature like a tree, rock, or sign.
Rocky Mountain underground (RMU) is a boutique snowboard manufacturer based in Colorado.
Snowboard slopestyle courses at the Olympics typically feature three jump sections and a jib section.
The 'backside 180' is one of the first rotational tricks most snowboarders learn.
Snowboard edges can be detuned (dulled) at the tip and tail to prevent catching.
Nicolas Müller is known for his fluid, artistic backcountry snowboarding style.
The 'crail grab' involves grabbing the nose with the rear hand.
Snowboard binding forward lean adjustment changes the angle of the highback for more or less response.
The World Snowboard Day is an international celebration held annually in December.
A 'pillow line' in backcountry snowboarding involves riding over snow-covered rocks and boulders.
The 'banana' or reverse camber profile was revolutionary when introduced in the mid-2000s.
Xavier de le Rue is a legendary French big-mountain snowboarder and Freeride World Tour champion.
A 'hardway' spin onto a rail means rotating in the more difficult direction to get on the feature.
Snowboard cross race courses are designed to be completed in about 30-40 seconds.
The 'double McTwist 1260' (also called the 'Tomahawk') was Shaun White's signature trick.
Snowboard film segments are often ranked by fans, with riders' 'parts' becoming career-defining.
Halfpipe dimensions for Olympic competition: 170 meters long, 22 feet high, and 66 feet wide.
The 'hand drag' is a stylish move where the rider touches the snow while turning at high speed.
Palmer Snowboards, founded by Shaun Palmer in 1995, was an influential 1990s brand.
Snowboarding was demonstrated at the 1994 Lillehammer Olympics but was not yet an official sport.
The 'stiffy' involves fully extending the legs during a grab to create a straight-legged pose.
Snowboard boot liners can be heat-molded for a custom fit using special ovens.
Ben Ferguson has become one of the most respected halfpipe riders with his creative approach.
The 'tuck knee' is a stylish variation where the front knee is tucked toward the board during a grab.
Snowboarding in Chile and Argentina offers Southern Hemisphere riding during North American summer.
The BRITS (British Snowboard and Freeski Championships) is the UK's premier snowsport event.
An 'alley-oop' is a trick performed in the uphill direction in the halfpipe.
Snowboard companies spend significant resources on research and development for new materials and shapes.
The 'slash' is a powerful carved turn that sprays snow, often used for dramatic effect.
Pat Moore is one of the hardest-charging backcountry snowboarders in the professional ranks.
Snowboard magazine 'Method' from Europe helped spread European snowboard culture globally.
A 'zero spin' is riding off a feature without any rotation, emphasizing grab and style.
The Snurfer had no bindings — riders held a rope attached to the nose for balance.
Rosa Khutor in Sochi, Russia hosted the snowboarding events at the 2014 Winter Olympics.
Snowboard base structures include different patterns that channel water for faster glide.
The 'misty flip' is an off-axis frontside rotation that includes an inverted element.
Snowboard parks employ dedicated crews to build, maintain, and shape features throughout the season.
Forum Snowboards was a prominent brand until it was discontinued by its parent company in 2012.
Chloe Kim scored a 98.25 in the 2018 Olympic halfpipe final, one of the highest scores ever.
The 'slob air' involves grabbing the toe edge with the front hand while the body faces downhill.
Snowboard racing has declined in popularity compared to freestyle and freeride disciplines.
A 'tree run' involves weaving between trees in gladed terrain, popular in areas with natural tree spacing.
The Resort Riders Alliance was formed by professional snowboarders to advocate for park and pipe quality.
Snowboard boots are universally softer than ski boots, which allows more ankle flex.
Yuki Kadono is a Japanese big air specialist known for massive backside triple corks.
A 'hip' is a terrain feature where two slopes meet at an angle, creating a natural launch.
Snowboard base material is ultra-high-molecular-weight polyethylene (UHMWPE).
The 'zeach' is a derogatory term for grabbing both the toe and heel edge at the same time.
Stevens Pass in Washington state has historically been a hub for progressive snowboarding.
Professional snowboard judges undergo extensive training and certification programs.
Marhar Snowboards, based in Michigan, is a small independent board maker known for creative designs.
The 'landing zone' or 'tranny' is the transition area where a rider touches down after a jump.
Snowboard specific avalanche airbag packs deploy a balloon to keep the rider near the surface during a slide.
The 'switch backside 1260' is a highly technical trick often seen in top-level slopestyle competition.
Korua Shapes is a European brand focused on directional powder and carving boards.
The progression from 900s to 1800s in competition has taken roughly 15 years.
Mt. Baker Banked Slalom is the oldest snowboard-only event, started in 1985 by Tom Sims.
A 'chatter' on a snowboard happens when the board vibrates on hard snow due to speed or stiff conditions.
Snowboard goggle lenses are color-coded: dark for sunny days, yellow or clear for flat light.
The 'McTwist' was first performed on a skateboard halfpipe by Mike McGill, hence the name.
Max Parrot won Olympic slopestyle gold at the 2022 Beijing Games after recovering from cancer.
Snowboard binding baseplate stiffness affects energy transfer and response.
The 'Japanese method' is a variation of the method where the board is pulled behind and above the head.
Snowboard cross first appeared in the Olympics at the 2006 Torino Games.
The 'over-under' rail setup has one rail directly above or below another.
DWD (Dinosaurs Will Die) Snowboards is an irreverent, rider-owned brand based in Quebec.
Snowboard competitions at the Olympics use a best-of-three-runs format in most events.
A 'flat box' is the most basic jib feature, with a flat sliding surface.
Juice Magazine and later online platforms documented the crossover between skateboarding, surfing, and snowboarding cultures.
The 'switch method' is a method air performed while riding switch, dramatically increasing difficulty.
Summit County, Colorado (home to Breck, Keystone, and Copper) is a major snowboarding hub.
Wrist guards with integrated splints can reduce snowboard wrist fracture severity by up to 85%.
The 'rainbow rail' is an arched rail feature that riders must navigate up and over.
Snowboard wax can be applied by hot iron, rub-on, or spray-on methods.
Scotty Vine and Justin Bennee have been influential in progressive urban snowboarding.
A 'spine' is a terrain feature where two transitions meet back-to-back, forming a ridge.
The first snowboard shop in the US opened in the early 1980s.
Snowboard binding toe straps have evolved from single straps to 3D-molded cap designs.
The 'chicken wing' is an uncomfortable-looking grab where the elbow goes above the arm line.
Mount Hood in Oregon offers year-round snowboarding on Palmer Glacier during summer.
Shaun White won his first X Games gold medal in 2003 at age 16.
Snowboard anti-icing sprays can help prevent snow from sticking to bindings and boots.
The 'hip jump' requires riders to launch off the side of a feature at an angle.
Elena Hight was the first woman to land a frontside 1080 in halfpipe competition.
Snowboarding in Japan has grown enormously, with Japanese riders winning multiple Olympic medals.
The 'underflip' is an off-axis rotation where the rider rotates under the axis of their spin.
Burton introduced the 'Custom' model in 1996, and it remains one of the best-selling snowboards ever.
A 'cat track' is a narrow, flat trail connecting different parts of a mountain, often tricky for snowboarders.
Snowboard tuning tools include edge tuners, wax scrapers, and nylon brushes.
The 'backflip' was once considered the most difficult trick but is now a baseline skill for professionals.
Snowboard Team Challenge events pit national teams against each other in combined formats.
The 'wheelie' involves riding on just the tail (manual) or nose (nosewheelie) without leaving the ground.
Travis Rice won the first-ever Natural Selection Tour event in Jackson Hole in 2021.
Snowboard impact vests protect vital organs and the spine during high-consequence riding.
The 'lien air' involves grabbing the heel edge with the front hand while the body leans forward.
A 'money booter' is a large jump designed for maximum amplitude and trick potential.
Snowboard competition bibs display the rider's number and are often collected as memorabilia.
Lib Tech was the first major brand to produce snowboards in the USA, in their Washington factory.
The 'quarter pipe' is a single halfpipe wall used for launching straight up.
Snowboarding requires different muscle groups than skiing, with more emphasis on core strength.
A 'spine transfer' involves jumping from one side of a spine and landing on the other.
Burton's 'Mystery Series' graphic boards became collectors' items in the early 2000s.
Snowboard competition courses at the Olympics are designed months in advance using 3D modeling.
The 'rocker-camber-rocker' profile combines float and edge hold in a hybrid design.
Snowboarder Kimmy Fasani has been a major advocate for women's progression in the sport.
The X Games 'Best in Snow' overall award recognizes the top snowboard performer across events.
Snowboard park laps refer to repeated runs through a terrain park for practice.
A 'cat' (snowcat) is a grooming vehicle that also accesses terrain for cat skiing and cat boarding.
The 'Pipe Dragon' is a specialized machine that shapes halfpipes to exact specifications.
Snowboard company Gentemstick, from Japan, handcrafts unique powder boards with surfboard-inspired shapes.
Snowboard binding canting adjusts the angle of the footbed to align with the rider's natural stance.
The term 'shredding' originated in surfing and was adopted by snowboarders to describe aggressive riding.
Snowboard competition formats continue to evolve, with rider-driven events gaining popularity over FIS formats.
A 'step-down' is a jump where the landing is lower than the takeoff.
Snowboard wax protects the base from oxidation and improves glide performance.
Mikey Rencz and Devun Walsh are among the most acclaimed video-part snowboarders.
The first World Snowboard Championships were held in Livigno, Italy in 1996.
Snowboard sidecut depth is the difference between the waist width and the nose/tail width.
The 'J-bar' and 'T-bar' surface lifts are notoriously difficult for snowboarders to ride.
Mons Røisland won Olympic big air gold at the 2022 Beijing Games for Norway.
Snowboard stomp pads were originally made from skateboard grip tape.
The Oakley Shaun White Signature Series goggles became some of the best-selling snow goggles ever.
A 'tomahawk' is an uncontrolled cartwheel crash where the rider tumbles end over end.
Troy, Vermont — where Jake Burton first manufactured boards — is a village of fewer than 100 people.
The first Olympic snowboard cross gold was won by Seth Wescott of the USA in 2006.
Snowboard edges are beveled at different angles for carving versus park riding.
The 'poptart' is an air-to-fakie in the halfpipe where the rider goes above the lip without a spin.
Snowboard companies often release limited-edition pro model boards with custom graphics each season.
Jibbing originated when snowboarders began riding urban handrails and ledges in the late 1990s.
The 'butter 360' combines a nose or tail press with a full flat-ground rotation.
Snowboard terrain parks are graded with S, M, L, and XL size designations for features.
Enni Rukajärvi of Finland has been a dominant force in women's slopestyle competitions.
Snowboard boots typically last one to two seasons of heavy use before packing out.
The 'heel drag' phenomenon occurs when a rider's heel or toe hangs over the edge of a too-narrow board.
Snowboard cross semifinals and finals use a four-rider format with the top two advancing.
A 'tabletop' jump has a flat section between the takeoff lip and landing zone for safety.
Kevin Pearce suffered a traumatic brain injury while training for the 2010 Olympics, documented in the film 'The Crash Reel.'
Snowboard companies often sponsor 'am' (amateur) riders who are developing toward professional status.
The 'speed check' is a quick turn to reduce speed before hitting a feature.
Snowboard helmet certifications include ASTM F2040 and CE EN 1077 safety standards.
Big air judging scores are based on difficulty, execution, amplitude, and landing.
Vermont was the birthplace of modern snowboarding, with Burton and several early resorts embracing the sport.
Snowboard cross courses must be wide enough for four to six riders racing side by side.
The 'scarecrow' trick involves a frontside 360 with a nose grab in the halfpipe.
Snowboard leash laws were implemented at resorts in the 1990s after several runaway board incidents.
Marcus Cleveland became the youngest X Games gold medalist in snowboarding at age 16.
